text.title: cicd-mtls-deployment Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for cicd-mtls-deployment. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Internal CA MUST issue short-lived client certificates (24h–7d) and MUST expose an ACME or API endpoint for automated renewal. Long-lived shared client certs (validity > 30d) shared across multiple services are forbidden — key compromise requires mass rotation with no revocation guarantee.
rule.rationale: Revocation (CRL/OCSP) is operationally hard to operate reliably at scale — clients cache CRL responses, OCSP responders are single points of failure, and many implementations silently accept certs if the OCSP check fails (soft-fail). Short-lived certs replace revocation with expiry as the primary security boundary.

rule.statement: nginx mTLS configurations MUST pass $ssl_client_s_dn (client Distinguished Name) and $ssl_client_verify to the backend via X-SSL-Client-DN and X-SSL-Client-Verify headers. The backend MUST validate $ssl_client_verify == SUCCESS before trusting the identity in X-SSL-Client-DN.
rule.rationale: Without passing $ssl_client_verify, the backend cannot distinguish between a verified client cert and an unauthenticated connection that somehow reached the proxy. A backend trusting X-SSL-Client-DN without checking the verify status is a critical authentication bypass.

rule.statement: When a service mesh is present (Linkerd, Istio, or Cilium), MUST NOT hand-roll mTLS by injecting client certs into application pods. The mesh's identity plane handles cert issuance, rotation, and revocation automatically per pod identity (SPIFFE/X.509 SVIDs). Hand-rolling on top of a mesh creates two competing PKI systems with conflicting root trust.
rule.rationale: Service mesh mTLS is transparent, automatic, and operates at the sidecar level with cryptographic identity bound to the pod service account. Adding application-level client certs on top introduces two PKIs with different roots, rotation schedules, and failure modes — all of which manifest as intermittent authentication failures that are extremely hard to diagnose.
